plainMeaning: Bowing to my teacher, I confess and sincerely repent for any disrespect I may have shown the guru today — in thought, word, or deed, or out of anger, pride, deceit, or greed — censuring myself and casting off the wrong.
recitation: āvassiāe, paḍikkamāmi, khamāsamaṇāṇaṁ devasiāe āsāyaṇāe, tittīsannayarāe, jaṁ kiṁci micchāe, maṇa-dukkaḍāe, vaya-dukkaḍāe, kāya-dukkaḍāe, kohāe, māṇāe, māyāe, lobhāe, savva-kāliāe, savva-micchovayārāe, savva-dhammāikkamaṇāe, āsāyaṇāe. jo me devasio aïāro kao, tassa khamāsamaṇo! paḍikkamāmi, nindāmi, garihāmi, appāṇaṁ vosirāmi.

wordByWord.gloss: with the āvaśyikī declaration (of having necessarily ceased/abandoned activity to make this confession)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit āvassiā < Sanskrit āvaśyikī, 'the obligatory/necessary-departure declaration'; instrumental-locative case ending -āe
wordByWord.notes: Twin of the naiṣedhikī motif of the sūtra: it opens the closing pratikramaṇa, marking the act as one of declared withdrawal from fault.

wordByWord.gloss: I turn back / I confess and repent
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit prati-kram-, 'to step back'; 1st person singular present
Prakrit khamā-samaṇa < Sanskrit kṣamā-śramaṇa; genitive plural -āṇaṁ
wordByWord.notes: Plural here: the reverence extends to the whole order of revered ascetics, not one guru only.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit daivasika, 'of the day'; instrumental-locative -āe
wordByWord.notes: The period-word that fixes which pratikramaṇa is underway (substituted in the fortnightly/seasonal/annual forms).

wordByWord.gloss: for/in respect of disrespect (āśātanā) shown to the guru
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit āśātanā, 'slight, affront'; instrumental-locative -āe
wordByWord.notes: Opens the list of what is being confessed; echoed again at the list's close.

wordByWord.etymology: tittīsa (Skt. trayastriṁśat, 'thirty-three') + annayara (Skt. anyatara, 'any one of'); case -āe
wordByWord.notes: Refers to the traditional enumeration of thirty-three modes of disrespect toward the guru.

wordByWord.etymology: jaṁ (Skt. yad) + kiṁci (Skt. kiṁcid); relative–indefinite pair, 'whatever'
wordByWord.notes: Sweeps in any fault not separately named.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit mithyā, 'false, wrong'; case -āe
maṇa (Skt. manas, 'mind') + dukkaḍa (Skt. duṣkṛta, 'ill-done'); case -āe
wordByWord.notes: First of the mind–speech–body triad.

wordByWord.etymology: vaya (Skt. vacas/vāk, 'speech') + dukkaḍa (duṣkṛta); case -āe
kāya (Skt. kāya, 'body') + dukkaḍa (duṣkṛta); case -āe
wordByWord.notes: Completes the triad of doors of action.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit koha < Sanskrit krodha; case -āe
wordByWord.notes: First of the four kaṣāyas named here as roots of disrespect.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit māṇa < Sanskrit māna; case -āe
Prakrit/Sanskrit māyā; case -āe
Prakrit/Sanskrit lobha; case -āe
wordByWord.notes: Last of the four passions.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit sarva; qualifies the following member of the compound
wordByWord.notes: Begins savva-kāliāe.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit kālika, 'temporal, of time'; case -āe — savva-kāliāe = 'pertaining to all time'
wordByWord.notes: Extends the confession beyond the day to faults of every time.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit sarva
wordByWord.notes: Begins savva-micchovayārāe.

wordByWord.gloss: by all false/insincere conduct (toward the guru)
wordByWord.etymology: micchā (Skt. mithyā) + uvayāra (Skt. upacāra, 'service, dealing') → micchovayāra, 'false treatment'; case -āe
wordByWord.notes: Sandhi of micchā + uvayāra.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit sarva
wordByWord.notes: Begins savva-dhammāikkamaṇāe.

wordByWord.gloss: by all transgression of dharma (right conduct)
wordByWord.etymology: dhamma (Skt. dharma) + aikkamaṇa (Skt. atikramaṇa, 'overstepping') → dharmātikramaṇa; case -āe
wordByWord.notes: Sandhi of dhamma + aikkamaṇa.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit āśātanā; case -āe
wordByWord.notes: Second occurrence: closes and gathers the whole enumerated list back under the heading of 'disrespect'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jo < Sanskrit yaḥ; relative pronoun, masculine singular
wordByWord.notes: Opens the summarizing relative clause.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit/Sanskrit me, enclitic of asmad, 'I'
Prakrit < Sanskrit daivasika; masculine nominative, agreeing with aïāro
wordByWord.notes: The period-word in adjectival form.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit aticāra, 'going beyond (a vow)'; masculine nominative
wordByWord.notes: Spelled with -y- (અતિયાર) elsewhere in this edition per its orthography.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit kṛta, past passive participle of kṛ-, 'to do'
Prakrit < Sanskrit tasya, genitive of tad
wordByWord.notes: Picks up the relative jo: 'whatever… of that…'

wordByWord.gloss: O kṣamāśramaṇa! (O forgiving one / revered guru)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit khamā-samaṇa < Sanskrit kṣamā-śramaṇa; vocative singular
wordByWord.notes: Direct address to the guru at the moment of repentance.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit prati-kram-; 1st person singular present
wordByWord.notes: First of the closing four-fold formula.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit/Sanskrit nind-, 'to blame'; 1st person singular present
wordByWord.notes: Self-censure before one's own conscience.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit garh-, 'to reproach, confess openly'; 1st person singular present
wordByWord.notes: Open confession in another's (the guru's) presence — distinct from inward nindā.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit appāṇa < Sanskrit ātman; accusative singular
wordByWord.notes: Object of vosirāmi: the fault-laden self/act being cast off.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit vi-ut-sṛj-, 'to release, abandon'; 1st person singular present
wordByWord.notes: The final act of release that completes the formula.

literalTranslation: With the declaration of cessation, I confess — concerning the day's disrespect toward the forgiving ascetics: any one of the thirty-three (āśātanās), whatever (was done) wrongly, by misdeed of mind, by misdeed of speech, by misdeed of body, by anger, by pride, by deceit, by greed, by (faults) of all time, by all false dealing, by all transgression of dharma — (all) as disrespect. Whatever day-transgression was committed by me, for that, O forgiving one, I turn back, I censure myself, I reproach myself, I renounce the (sinful) self.
idiomaticTranslation: Having drawn back from all activity to make this amends, I confess every disrespect I have shown my revered teachers in the course of this day. Whatever of the thirty-three slights I committed, whatever wrong I did, by mind, by speech, by body; out of anger, pride, deceit or greed; through faults of any time, through all insincere conduct, through any overstepping of right conduct — for all such disrespect: O forgiving one, whatever lapse of the day I am guilty of, that I now turn back from, that I censure within myself, that I confess openly before you, and that errant self I cast away.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the preceding unit only *asked* for pardon (khāmemi… vaikkamaṁ), this concluding unit performs the full repentance. It is built as one long instrumental list framed by āsāyaṇāe at both ends: the opening āsāyaṇāe announces the subject (disrespect to the guru), and the second āsāyaṇāe seals it, so that everything between — the thirty-three slights, the mind–speech–body misdeeds, the four passions, and the sweeping 'all time / all false dealing / all overstepping of dharma' — is gathered up as varieties of a single fault. The drafting is deliberately exhaustive: named categories (tittīsannayarāe, the three dukkaḍas, the four kaṣāyas) are followed by catch-all sweeps (jaṁ kiṁci, the three savva- compounds) so that nothing escapes. Then the relative clause jo me devasio aïāro kao narrows the cosmic list back to the concrete: 'whatever lapse of *the day* I actually committed.' The verse climaxes in the graded four-verb formula paḍikkamāmi–nindāmi–garihāmi–vosirāmi: I step back from the fault, I blame it inwardly, I confess it openly before the guru, and finally I release it. Note the precise difference the verse preserves between nindā (private self-reproach) and garhā (avowal in another's presence) — the guru is addressed by name, khamāsamaṇo, exactly at the hinge between inward and outward confession. The closing appāṇaṁ vosirāmi does not renounce the soul itself but the fault-bound self, completing veneration as an act of self-purification, not mere courtesy.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse enacts the structure of right repentance (samyak pratikramaṇa): recognition, three channels of action (yoga — mind, speech, body), the four passions (kaṣāya) as the inner cause, and a graded movement from turning-back to renunciation. Its specific doctrinal weight is that disrespect to the guru is itself treated as a confessable transgression with its own catalogue (the thirty-three āśātanās), underscoring that reverence for the enlightened teacher is not sentiment but a vow whose breach incurs karmic lapse. The four-verb close is the same canonical formula used to dispose of fault elsewhere in the Āvaśyaka liturgy, applied here to the guru-relationship.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter uses this verse to convert a vague feeling of 'I may have been disrespectful' into a precise, owned act: name the channel (was it a thought, a word, a gesture?), name the root passion (irritation? ego?), then move deliberately through the four steps rather than stopping at apology. The doubled āsāyaṇāe is a cue to let the long list resolve into one honest admission before closing with release.

crossReferences.text: Vandanaka (Guru-vandana) Āvaśyaka — closing pratikramaṇa of guru-āśātanā
The four-fold formula paḍikkamāmi nindāmi garihāmi (appāṇaṁ) vosirāmi, paralleling its use in Iriyāvahiyam / Tassa Uttari
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

sources.title: Suguru Vandana Sutra — text and English translation
sources.consultedFor: Verifying the running English sense of the closing pratikramaṇa (āśātanā, four passions, and the nindāmi–garihāmi–vosirāmi formula)

sources.title: Suguru Vandana Sutra — Jain Sutras and Stotras
sources.consultedFor: Cross-checking the rendering of appāṇaṁ vosirāmi as renunciation of the fault-self and the day-transgression clause

sources.title: Suguru Vandana Sutra (ritual texts collection)
sources.consultedFor: Independent transliteration and meaning of the closing unit

sources.title: H. D. T. Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Sanskrit Dictionary)
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit-to-Sanskrit glosses of āsāyaṇā, dukkaḍa, aïāra, vosir-, garih-, and the -āe instrumental ending

notes: āsāyaṇāe occurs twice and functions as a frame (announce-then-seal); both are glossed as 'disrespect (āśātanā).' jaṁ kiṁci is treated as a two-word relative–indefinite pair though printed solid. micchovayāra and dhammāikkamaṇa are read as the sandhi-compounds mithyā-upacāra and dharma-atikramaṇa respectively. 'devasio/devasiāe' is the daily period-word, swapped for pakkhiya/cāumāsiya/saṁvacchariya in the other pratikramaṇas (noted only to place the word, not to restate the rule). The thirty-three āśātanās are a standard enumeration of the Śvetāmbara Āvaśyaka commentary tradition; the specific count is taken as authoritative per the knowledge base, but the per-item list is not printed in this verse. Online translations consulted are devotional renderings, not critical editions; the per-token glosses rest on standard Prakrit lexicography rather than a page-level critical citation.
